text: The surgeon enters the abdomen through the palmar point and places lateral ports, revealing extensive omental and bowel adhesions stretching from the xiphoid process to the pelvis.
The surgeon begins adhesiolysis using sharp scissor dissection to carefully separate extensive omental and bowel adhesions within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon ensures close proximity to the abdominal wall while allowing the pneumoperitoneum to create space through pneumodissection, facilitating the separation of adhesions.
The surgeon begins adhesiolysis using harmonic shears, targeting the extensive omental and bowel adhesions visible from the xiphoid process to the pelvis.
The surgeon maintains close proximity to the anterior abdominal wall during the adhesiolysis procedure, ensuring precise dissection near the abdominal surface.
The surgeon identifies small herniated defects and previous sutures within the abdominal cavity, visible amidst extensive omental and bowel adhesions.

The surgeon observes the underlying bowel that was previously sutured, revealing a large herniated defect. Using gentle traction, counter traction, and rotatory movements, the bowel contents are carefully reduced back into the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon proceeds cautiously to avoid sudden movements that could cause bleeding from the underlying bowel, maintaining close proximity to the anterior abdominal wall during the procedure.
In case there is a bleeding we can control it using bipolar or harmonic shears.
We have to keep on adjusting our trochars and ports accordingly.
The surgeon observes the bowel densely adhering to the abdominal wall, highlighting the need for careful dissection to separate the structures.
The surgeon uses sharp cutting scissors to carefully dissect near the bowel, avoiding the use of energy sources, while maintaining proximity to the anterior abdominal wall.
The video shows the underlying bowel densely adherent to previous sutures, highlighting the extent of adhesion and the challenging nature of the dissection.
Again, very, very slow and meticulous dissection is required and patience is very, very important while contemplating an adduciolysis of this level.
The herniated defect is visible, showing a protrusion through the abdominal wall.
During the adhesiolysis, the surgeon encounters several enterotomies, which are repaired laparoscopically with sutures directly at the site.

I would like to emphasize that while if there is some endotomy that is encountered, so just stop with the IDC lysis and then do the repair immediately. The reason being is we do not lose track of the enterotomy and then a proper traction which is there by the previous additions can be obtained. Then after closing the enterotomy, I continue the edysulisis.
The surgeon visualizes another herniated defect and carefully reduces the herniated contents back into the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon begins to appreciate the adhesions extending from the upper to lower regions, with visible defects in the tissue.

The surgeon observes the previous sutures and bites taken with the underlying bowel, leading to significant bowel adhesions. A large enterotomy is visible, and the surgeon uses 3-0 Vicryl to close the enterotomy defect with intermittent sutures.
We have to be careful that we do not take very deep bites in order to create a future structure in the bobble.
The surgeon continues sharp adhesiolysis, carefully separating adhesions while managing two previously encountered enterotomies.
The surgeon completes the adhesiolysis procedure, reaching the pelvic region where the adhesions have been fully separated.

The video shows a panoramic view of the surgical field after adhesiolysis, revealing multiple Swiss cheese-like defects and previous suture bites on the underlying bowel. The bowel appears closed with visible trotteries.
The surgeon uses non-absorbable barbed sutures to close the defect, avoiding mesh placement due to the presence of enterotomies.

in these cases a laparoscopic anatomical repair of the hernia would suffice and we have to explain to the patients that in the case of recurrence we might put a mesh and that is the very prudent and a very important decision that has to be explained to the patient and to the patient's attenders.
The surgeon inspects the closed hernia defects for hemostasis, ensuring no active bleeding is present, marking the completion of the procedure.
Thank you for watching.
